currentUse: Active Catholic church — St. Peter Lithuanian Parish, 75 Flaherty Way, South Boston, operating within the South Boston Seaport Catholic Collaborative (with St. Monica); weekly Lithuanian-language Mass continues
summary: St. Peter Lithuanian Parish in South Boston is alive and functioning as of July 2026 — the rare Lithuanian parish in America that survived inside its diocese. The 1904 brick church at 75 Flaherty Way remains in active Catholic use, with Sunday Mass at 9:00 a.m. in English and 10:30 a.m. in Lithuanian followed by a parish-hall brunch. The Archdiocese of Boston's directory still lists Saint Peter as a distinct parish "in collaborative with Saint Monica" (the South Boston Seaport Catholic Collaborative, formed under Disciples in Mission effective June 2, 2015), with Rev. William M. Kennedy as pastor. Lithuanian ministry is organized through the Lithuanian Apostolate of the Archdiocese of Boston, which publishes a monthly bilingual bulletin — current through July 2026 — under directors Aldona Lingertat and Gloria Adomkaitis with administrator Daiva Navickas. On October 20, 2024 the parish celebrated its 120th anniversary with a Mass by Bishop Cristiano Barbosa, Vicar General of the archdiocese, attended by former Mayor Raymond Flynn and Councillor Ed Flynn, with written greetings from Cardinal O'Malley. The community survived a 2004 closure threat (protested with a Hill of Crosses at the church entrance) and in 2022 mounted a defense against a proposed merger, and a plaque reading "St. Peter Church, Built by Lithuanian immigrants 1904" was added to the facade in 2022. The proposed 2021-22 canonical merger has not visibly been executed: the archdiocesan directory continues to list the parish as its own entity.

developments.headline: Parish active with July 2026 bulletin; Lithuanian Mass continues weekly
developments.detail: The Lithuanian Apostolate of the Archdiocese of Boston (c/o SBSCatholic, P.O. Box 270799, So. Boston) published its July 2026 bilingual bulletin, listing the weekend Lithuanian Mass at St. Peter Lithuanian Parish, 75 Flaherty Way, Sundays 10:30 a.m., Mass intentions through July, an advisory board of ten members, directors Aldona Lingertat and Gloria Adomkaitis, and administrator Daiva Navickas. It asks supporters to give via sbscatholic.org/giving and highlights parishioners dancing with Boston Sambūris at the XVII North American Lithuanian Folk Dance Festival in Chicago.

sources.title: Lithuanian Apostolate bulletin, Liepa/July 2026
sources.publisher: St. Peter Lithuanian Parish / Lithuanian Apostolate of the Archdiocese of Boston

developments.headline: Archdiocesan directory lists Saint Peter as a distinct parish in collaborative with Saint Monica
developments.detail: The Boston archdiocesan directory (thebostonpilot.com) lists Saint Peter, South Boston as a parish 'Collaborative with Saint Monica,' address 50 Orton Marotta Way, with Rev. William M. Kennedy as pastor, Rev. Barnabas Chukwuma Nkwuda as vicar, and Rev. Jude Anyanwu in residence; Masses: Sunday 9:00 a.m. English, 10:30 a.m. Lithuanian, Thursday 9:00 a.m. weekday. The continued separate listing indicates the merger proposed in 2021-22 has not been consummated as of this check.

sources.title: Saint Peter - South Boston (archdiocesan parish directory)

developments.headline: 120th anniversary Mass celebrated by Vicar General Bishop Cristiano Barbosa
developments.detail: Parishioners filled St. Peter's on Flaherty Way for a Mass marking 120 years since the church's 1904 consecration, celebrated by Bishop Cristiano Barbosa, Vicar General of the Archdiocese of Boston, with Pastor Fr. William Kennedy; former Mayor Raymond Flynn and Councillor Ed Flynn attended, and written greetings came from Cardinal O'Malley, Congressman Lynch, and state legislators. Administrator Daiva Navickas presented a parish history, and the parish announced its Autumn Bazaar for November 24. The Boston Pilot marked the anniversary with a history by archdiocesan archivist Thomas Lester recounting the 1899 chapel fire, the 1901 brick church on West Fifth Street, and the 1904 blessing under Fr. Jonas Zilinskas.

sources.title: St. Peter Lithuanian Catholic Church 120 Years Old
A home for Lithuanian Catholics in Boston (Thomas Lester)

developments.headline: Commemorative plaque added to facade amid merger fight
developments.detail: A plaque reading 'St. Peter Church, Built by Lithuanian immigrants 1904' was added to the building's facade in 2022. The same source records the church surviving the 2004 closure wave after parishioners erected a Hill of Crosses at the entrance in protest, and that the church remains open. In February 2022 the Lithuanian Apostolate sought and received assurances from Auxiliary Bishop Peter Uglietto that Lithuanian liturgy, customs and traditions would continue under the then-proposed merger.

sources.title: Boston, Massachusetts: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage
Dear Friends of the Lithuanian Apostolate, February 2022 (bulletin)

developments.headline: Parish entered Disciples in Mission collaborative, retaining its own identity
developments.detail: Under the archdiocese's Disciples in Mission plan, St. Peter was grouped effective June 2, 2015 into a collaborative with St. Vincent de Paul and St. Monica/St. Augustine; Fr. Steponas Žukas stepped down as sole pastor and the parishes came to share clergy, with at least one Sunday Mass (in Lithuanian) guaranteed to continue at St. Peter. This collaborative — now branded the South Boston Seaport Catholic Collaborative — is the structure the parish still operates within in 2026.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions; several refinements. (1) Archive status 'standing' is confirmed — the parish is active with bulletins through July 2026 and a weekly Lithuanian Mass. (2) The archive's '2021-22 merger deferred / refused merger consent Feb 2022' is consistent with what I found: the Feb 2022 Lithuanian Apostolate letter documents the merger fight, and as of July 2026 the archdiocesan directory still lists Saint Peter as a distinct parish 'in collaborative with Saint Monica' — i.e., the merger has not visibly been executed, though I found no explicit announcement that it was formally cancelled versus indefinitely deferred. (3) Refinement: the parish has operated inside a Disciples-in-Mission collaborative since June 2, 2015 (with St. Vincent de Paul and St. Monica/St. Augustine), a shared-clergy arrangement distinct from the later merger proposal — the archive's 'survived inside the diocese' should be read as survival as a parish within this collaborative. (4) Founding: archive says 1896; Global True Lithuania agrees (established 1896), while the Pilot history dates the first brick church to 1901 and the current church's blessing to 1904 — consistent, not conflicting. (5) The archive's 1904 deed-cession-for-a-priest claim and 'only Lithuanian church in New England' claim were not independently confirmed or contradicted by sources I opened.
gaps: Could not open sbscatholic.org (HTTP 403), so the collaborative's own current framing of St. Peter is unverified first-hand; a search snippet attributed to that site says St. Peter and St. Monica-St. Augustine currently host ministries of the St. Vincent de Paul Parish community 'while that Church is being evaluated' — a possible sign of ongoing collaborative-level restructuring pressure that I could not confirm by opening the page. No explicit archdiocesan statement was found on the final disposition of the 2021-22 merger proposal (cancelled vs. dormant). The clergy 'presiding priest' lines in the July 2026 bulletin are marked TBD and July weekday Lithuanian Masses were noted as celebrated in the St. Peter rectory rather than the church — I found no explanation (this may simply reflect summer scheduling or clergy rotation). No property-sale question applies since the parish never closed; no real-estate listings or sale records were found or sought beyond confirming active church use. The parish website's events page is stale (last events ~2020), so current community activity is documented via the monthly bulletins instead.
